content:
About # PC1-CATS (Collaboration Across Heterogeneous Spaces) aims to design collaboration spaces that enable continuous and seamless collaboration, bridging remote and face-to-face work, tightly coupled and loosely coupled interactions, as well as group collaborations involving spontaneous subgroups or side interactions.
The objective is to address changes of scale along four key dimensions:
Diversity of users Diversity of interaction devices/modalities Complexity of datasets, tasks and environments Handling the dynamic transitions between collaboration spaces The expected breakthrough is the definition of mixed collaboration spaces that place users fully in control while providing rich, flexible, and multi-user experiences.
Since 2023, PC1 has funded 29 PhD students (15 fully funded, 14 partially supported), 2 postdoctoral researchers, and 11 internships.
Links # PC1 on the PEPR eNSEMBLE homepage: https://www.pepr-ensemble.fr/cats-espaces-de-collaboration/ Acknowledgement # This work was supported by a French government grant managed by the Agence Nationale de la Recherche as part of the France 2030 program, reference ANR-22-EXEN-0002 (PEPR eNSEMBLE / PC1 CATS).
title: PC1-CATS: COLLABORATIVE SPACES

content: Directors # Jean-Marie BURKHARDT Maud MARCHAL Christian SANDOR Marcos SERRANO PostDocs # 2025 # Flavien LEBRUN Wen-Jie TSENG PhD Students # 2025 # Zeineb Amara Fehmi Cem BOYACIOGLU Jean-Charles BRUNET Ramakrishnan KUMARAVELU Alyssa LEMOINE Juliette Meunier 2024 # Constance BAULT Julien BERRY Majd BITAR Mattéo BORREGO Clémence CUNIN Francesco DETTORI Thomaz FEVRE Clémentine GUILLEMET Yawen LI Hovhannes MARGARYAN Sergio Camilo Medina Galvis Frederick Salin Guillaume Vallet Frederick Vickery Mathilde Yousefi Xinpei Zheng 2023 # Manel BOUKLI-HACENE Lucas BRAND Victor BRÉHAULT Tristan LANNUZEL Juliette LE MEUDEC David Maruscsak Sabrina Toofany
title: People

content:
Postdoc Researcher at Sorbonne Université, ISIR City: Paris Starting Year: 2026 Grant: Fully funded by PC1 Project # Maintaining Physical Collaboration in Virtual Reality
Personal Website # https://wenjietseng.github.io/
title: Wen-Jie TSENG

content: A testing post for posting PC1 activities…
title: Testing Post

content:
PhD student at Université de Lille, CRIStAL, MINT City: Lille Starting Year: 2025 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0000-0302-0638 Supervisor: Florent Berthaut, Tifanie Bouchara Thesis Title # PARMI – Personalised and Accessible extended Reality Musical Instruments for Practitioners-Patients Collaboration in Music Therapy
Abstract # My thesis aims to investigate the co-design of personalized, accessible, and collaborative virtual musical instruments in extended reality that would enable the practitioners to adapt the instruments and guide and support their autistic children patients during collective music-making sessions. Our goal is to foster synchronous, asymmetric and heterogeneous collaboration among users with varied psycho-cognitive profiles, ensuring that musical creation is a shared experience between the therapist and the child. A multidisciplinary approach will be adopted combining approaches from computer science (human-computer interaction, extended reality) and approaches inspired from human sciences (psychology, cognitive ergonomics) to design such immersive interfaces for collective musical expression.
title: Alyssa LEMOINE

content:
PhD student at Télécom Paris, LTCI, DIVA City: Paris Starting Year: 2025 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8066-0759Supervisor: Eric Lecolinet, Daniel Medeiros, Marcos Serrano Thesis Title # Asymmetric Remote Collaboration for Physical Therapy and Implications on Rehabilitation/Movement Guidance
Abstract # The project investigates asymmetric remote collaboration for physical rehabilitation, addressing the limitations of current single-user Extended Reality (XR) systems that lack personalized therapist oversight. While home-based automated feedback exists, it often fails to provide the specific guidance necessary to prevent injury or correct form effectively. This thesis proposes a mixed-reality framework where the therapist is virtually transported into the patient’s environment, aiming to restore the critical rapport and real-time guidance found in clinical settings. The primary objective is to determine optimal user representations and explore multimodal interaction techniques, specifically integrating multimodal feedback to facilitate motor learning. Unlike general remote collaboration, physiotherapy requires a distinct focus on the patient’s biomechanics rather than the virtual environment itself. Through iterative prototyping and user studies with physiotherapists and patients, this work aims to derive design guidelines for remote guidance systems. Ultimately, the study evaluates the extent to which remote XR collaboration can effectively complement or substitute traditional in-person rehabilitation for patients with limited mobility.
title: Fehmi Cem BOYACIOĞLU

content:
PhD student at Nantes Université, LS2N, PACCE City: Nantes Starting Year: 2025 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/nan Supervisor: Yannick Prié Thesis Title # Activités collectives dans les télé-cliniques immersives pour la prévention en santé mentale
Abstract # My thesis is about collective activities in immersive meta-clinics for mental health prevention. The goal is to study the tools and process needed to improve the experience of (therapeutic) group discussions and group activities in VR
title: Jean-Charles BRUNET

content:
PhD student at Inria Center at the University of Bordeaux, POTIOC City: Bordeaux Starting Year: 2025 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0007-0630-8945Supervisor: Fabien Lotte, Arnaud Prouzeau, Sébastien Rimbert Thesis Title # Augmenting Workspace Awareness with Passive BCI: From Monitoring to Enhancing Team Coordination
Abstract # While existing tools can support workspace awareness by monitoring observable actions, they have trouble capturing the underlying cognitive process essential for effective collaboration, particularly in situations of loose coupling or misalignment. This PhD project aims to investigate whether passive Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) based on electroencephalography (EEG) can be used to identify neurophysiological markers that reflect different levels of collaborative coupling, ranging from loose to tight, as well as situations of mutual misunderstanding. The project will first explore the existence of such EEG markers in validated collaborative tasks. It will also evaluate the stability of these markers across different experimental paradigms, and across participants and time, known as sources of variability in EEG and BCI performance. If successful, the project will explore how these markers can be used as real-time neurofeedback to collaborators, to enhance their workspace awareness and reduce misalignment.
title: Juliette MEUNIER

content:
PhD student at Centre Inria de l’Université de Lille, Loki City: Lille Starting Year: 2025 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0004-8174-338X Supervisor: Thomas Pietrzak, Bruno Fruchard, Donald Degraen Thesis Title # Investigating Benefits and Limitations of Asymmetric Collaboration for Designing Sensory Experiences in Mixed Reality
Abstract # Immersive technologies, such as Virtual Reality (VR), support creating virtual worlds to simulate various scenarios. Tactile and visual experiences can increase the sense of immersion and make virtual objects tangible. Designing them is challenging and requires to be immersed in the virtual environment, which is tiring and limits the kind of parameters controlled. Designers must therefore often switch back-and-forth between the virtual environment and a desktop computer to fine-tune the experience. This project studies the benefits and limitations of asymmetric collaboration to design such sensory experiences, i.e., one user is immersed in the virtual environment to feel the experiences and design them in-situ while a collaborator controls its parameters in more details on a desktop computer, as opposed to a single user switching back-and-forth between the two setups. Our goal is to identify whether collaborative setups can leverage the benefits of each environment while minimizing their respective limitation
title: Ramakrishnan  KUMARAVELU

content:
PhD student at IMT Atlantique, LabSTICC, INUIT City: Brest Starting Year: 2025 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0000-5988-5067 Supervisor: Thierry Duval, Cédric Fleury Thesis Title # Exploring alternative problem-solving approaches for the factory of the future using virtual and augmented reality
Abstract # My PhD thesis title is “Exploring alternative problem-solving approaches using virtual and augmented reality for the factory of the future”, its subject consists of using Augmented Reality (AR) by an operator and Virtual Reality (VR) by an expert to collaborate remotely on a problem solving task. The expert explores different approaches to solve a problem faced by an operator in the industrial field using specific visual cues, real-time synchronized digital twins and real-time exchange between the operator and the expert to explore the problem and propose a solution that takes into account the real conditions of the operator and the feasibility of the proposed solution.
title: Zeineb AMARA

content:
PhD student at Telecom Paris, LTCI, IDIA City: Paris Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0004-8689-3168 Supervisor: Eric Lecolinet, Daniel Medeiros, Samuel Huron Thesis Title # Remote Collaboration Strategies for Everyday Public Spaces
Abstract # My thesis explores how extended reality (XR) technologies transform social interaction, collaboration, and privacy when immersive systems are used in everyday, shared spaces. Rather than treating XR as an isolated virtual experience, my research examines XR as a situated practice embedded in environments such as offices, cafés, parks, libraries, and transportation settings, where social norms, spatial expectations, and bystander relationships already exist.
Using qualitative and design-oriented methods—including scenario-based workshops, interviews, and speculative design probes—I study how XR reconfigures visibility, attention, and social legibility among both users and non-users. One line of work focuses on collaborative XR, identifying tensions around trust, coordination, and legitimacy when immersive work becomes socially opaque or spatially disruptive. A second line investigates bystander privacy and privacy negotiation, examining how recording, sensing, and data capture in XR introduce new forms of implicit and explicit social negotiation in public and semi-public spaces.
Across these studies, the thesis develops a conceptual framework grounded in theories of social space to connect empirical findings with design implications. Overall, the research aims to inform the design of XR systems that support effective collaboration while enabling context-sensitive privacy negotiation and socially acceptable immersive interaction in everyday environments.
title: Yawen LI

content:
PhD student at IMT Atlantique, Lab-STICC, INUIT City: Brest Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0002-1825-3893 Supervisor: Thierry Duval, Cédric Fleury Thesis Title # Édition et synchronisation du Jumeau Numérique en réalité virtuelle et réalité augmentée pour l’usine du futur
Abstract # This PhD thesis focuses on the 3D authoring of a digital twin in augmented reality and virtual reality for the industry of the future. This research project is part of a national project: 5G Metaverse. The authoring task can be divided into three subtasks: (1) the selection to define the subparts of the 3D models, (2) the positioning of the segmented parts to match the physical twin, and (3) the behavior creation to define movements of parts. Three main challenges have been found to enable end-users, such as operators, technicians, or engineers, to easily author 3D models: (1) asymmetrical synchronous collaboration between an in-situ worker and a remote expert, (2) usability for end-users as they are not 3D authoring experts, and (3) cross-platform ability to suit remote immersive collaboration. Our first study will compare three different interaction techniques (1) on a desktop or (2) in an immersive environment using a virtual reality headset, to find the most suitable technique for a specified platform for a selection task. The next study will focus on collaborative authoring using the best techniques identified in the first study.
title: Clémence CUNIN

content:
PhD student at Sorbonne Université, ISIR City: Paris Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0009-1092-7751 Supervisor: Ludovic Saint-Bauzel Thesis Title # Apprentissage à travers la relation kinesthésique et la synchronisation cérébrale
Abstract # In collaborative settings, pairs exchange information through multiple sensory modalities (visual, auditory) to improve task efficiency. While the roles of sight and sound are well-documented, the influence of kinesthetic cues remains under-explored. My PhD project aims to fill this gap and proposes to inspect the influence of the physical link, which translates into an exchange of haptic information, in the effectiveness of dyadic collaboration. To this end, we subject pairs of individuals to collaborative movement tasks by means of a robotic system allowing an exchange of haptic information only. We rely on measures of brain synchronization between two individuals, an emerging metric in the field of interaction neuroscience, and established to be related to the quality of collaboration. In addition, we propose to assess the sense of joint agency, defined as a conscious sensation of acting jointly/having joint control in an action, which is informative of collaboration quality. A better understanding of the mechanisms by which collaboration emerges and can be optimized will inform models applicable to automatic devices.
title: Clémentine GUILLEMET

content:
PhD student at LAMIH UMR CNRS 8201, Université Polytechnique Hauts de France City: Valenciennes Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0005-9464-8995 Supervisor: Sophie Lepreux, Christophe Kolski Thesis Title # Analyse et conception des interactions et des collaborations nécessaires pour assister la mobilité piétonne
Abstract # Mobility (walking) is an essential part of everyday life. However, it can be difficult for some people, particularly those with intellectual disabilities. There are around 900,000 people with intellectual disabilities in France, who experience difficulties with intellectual and adaptive functions. Assistive tools such as Google Maps can be useful, but are often too complex and do not provide personalised assistance. In order to help people with intellectual disabilities with their mobility, and more specifically with wayfinding, this thesis aims to study solutions for collaboration with one or more remote persons. This collaboration could prove particularly useful in unexpected situations encountered by people with intellectual disabilities (e.g. a cancelled bus or an inaccessible route). The carer, whether professional or not, must be able to understand where the person in difficulty is and help them either find a new route or put them in touch with other people. Collaboration will therefore take place between carers and with the person with an intellectual disability in order to involve them in finding a solution. The aim is to assess the acceptance and effectiveness of solutions according to profiles and configurations.
title: Constance BAULT

content:
PhD student at Université Paris-Saclay, LISN, ARAI City: Paris Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0008-8482-7502 Supervisor: Huyen Nguyen, Christian Sandor Thesis Title # Empathetic storytelling in interactive extended reality
Abstract # Exhibition lighting design requires collaboration among curators, conservators, scenographers, lighting designers, and technicians. Through interviews and workshops with museum professionals, we identified three challenges in current practice. First, stakeholders describe light differently, from narrative descriptions to technical specifications, yet these perspectives must eventually converge into a unified design. Second, design decisions evolve faster than supporting documentation, causing teams to work from outdated references. Third, selecting appropriate lighting fixtures requires extensive trial and error, slowing the transition from concept to implementation. We propose a pre-visualization framework that generates accurate lighting effects directly onto photographs of the exhibition space. The system accepts sketches, reference images, and natural language as input, allowing stakeholders to express lighting ideas in familiar terms. By providing a shared visual reference accessible without specialized software or expertise, the system enables collective evaluation before physical installation. This approach aims to reduce miscommunication, accelerates decision-making, and ensures that final lighting configurations reflect input from all collaborating parties.
title: Francesco DETTORI

content:
PhD student at INSA Rennes, Inria, Univ Rennes, IRISA City: Rennes Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0009-7632-3758 Supervisor: Valérie Gouranton, Florent Berthaut, Ronan Gaugne Thesis Title # Environnements Immersifs Partagés pour la Co-Création Artistique en Modalités Hétérogènes
Abstract # My research investigates how immersive extended reality (XR) environments can support multidisciplinary artistic creation through embodied interaction. While collaborative artistic practices such as music, dance, and theatre increasingly rely on digital tools, existing systems often remain discipline-specific and impose technical constraints that hinder creative exchange. This thesis explores how shared immersive environments can act as mediators between artistic modalities, enabling new forms of collaboration.
The research introduces S.P.A.R.K, a virtual reality prototype that enables asynchronous interaction between prerecorded dance movements and sound generation. Rather than relying on real-time motion capture, the system employs spatially distributed interaction objects that map movement data to audiovisual parameters via OSC, enabling integration with established tools such as Pure Data and Max/MSP. This asynchronous workflow allows artists to work independently while contributing to a coherent creative process.
The methodology follows a user-centered, iterative design approach involving professional and amateur artists through workshops and controlled user studies. Quantitative measures, including usability and creativity support questionnaires, are combined with qualitative analyses of user behavior and interviews to ground the design process in artists’ practices and collaborative needs. The research aims to contribute design insights for XR systems supporting artistic collaboration and multimodal interaction.
title: Frederick SALIN

content:
PhD student at Bretagne INP ENIB, Lab-STICC UMR 6285, INUIT City: Brest Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0008-7704-1771 Supervisor: Ronan Querrec, Sebastien Kubicki, Nassim Mokhtari Thesis Title # Détection des (inter)actions d’un opérateur distant assisté en Réalité Augmentée et collaborant avec un expert local en Réalité Virtuelle pour de l’assistance en milieux isolés : application à la médecine spatiale
Abstract # My thesis aims to detect and recognise the (inter)actions performed by an isolated operator during an Augmented Reality Guided Procedure using various forms of input. These inputs range from high level semantic information about the procedure and scene to low level system data collected by sensors. The semantic information will be provided through an environmental knowledge and human activity representation meta-model (Application Domain Model), which can provide the states, attributes and current possible actions to be performed by the isolated operator. Additional data is collected through devices such as a Kinect, connected objects, and infrared cameras, while information about the virtual environment is gathered by the underlying simulation platform.
In order to detect and recognise the (inter)actions performed, this information is then provided to Human Activity Recognition models in order to deduce the current activity performed within the context.
The application of this topic is that of a medical procedure in an isolated and potentially asynchronous space, where an operator (ranging from an astronaut on Mars, to a soldier at sea), guided by AR information, is collaborating with a medical expert in a virtual representation of the isolated environment.
title: Frederick VICKERY

content:
PhD student at CRISTAL City: Lille Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0005-1461-7989 Supervisor: Florent Berthaut, Ronan Gaugne, Valérie Gouranton Thesis Title # Performances Artistiques Immersives Collaboratives
Abstract # My thesis consists of designing ways for artists to collaborate during immersive artistic performances, or between artists and audiences. This may involve creating new scenographies for immersive scenes that facilitate a sense of co-presence (the feeling of sharing a virtual environment with someone) among the audience in a shared space. This is the case in my first work on a scenography using a method of locomotion known as “spatial compartmentalization,” which allows the audience to explore a performance rather than watch it passively. The physical space is compartmentalized by virtual zones in which there is a viewpoint on a virtual environment larger than the physical space. Access to these zones is achieved by walking, which ensures spatial synchronization between all members of the audience. But my subject also allows me to work on methods of asymmetric (or non-asymmetric) and asynchronous (or non-asynchronous) interaction between the audience and artists, as well as the creation of new interaction devices to give artists more expressiveness during performances.
title: Guillaume VALLET

content:
PhD student at LISN - Paris Saclay City: Paris Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0004-2302-0474 Supervisor: Christian Sandor, Quentin Bammey Thesis Title # Enabling Subtle Visual Modifications with AR+AI
Abstract # No abstract available.
title: Hovhannes MARGARYAN

content:
PhD student at University Paris-Saclay, LISN, ILDA City: Paris Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0003-2034-4820 Supervisor: Caroline Appert, Olivier Chapuis Thesis Title # [THandgibles] Approche Centrée sur la Main pour l’Interaction Tangible Collaborative
Abstract # In AR, the tracking of physical objects is important to integrate and use their tangible potential to display or interact with the AR system.
I aim to create an opportunistic system usuable in any situations to track object without training. I focus on the user’s hands to gather informations about objects and avoid common errors found in object tracking systems. Users can grasp object to register their position in the system which will then detect when they are manipulated to update the object data.
This lightweight system is designed to be simple to use and compatible with collaborative situations. I aim to develop more potential use cases by integrating useful widgets and potentially computer vision to create useful tools.
title: Julien BERRY

content:
PhD student at Nantes Université City: Nantes Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0007-2725-8334 Supervisor: Yannick Prié, Jean-Philippe Rivière, Rebecca Fribourg Thesis Title # Maintenir la collaboration pendant les transtions en réalité virtuelle sociale
Abstract # With Virtual Reality (VR) becoming more available for commercial use, social VR platforms such as VRChat, RecRooms, Meta Horizon Worlds emerged rapidly, shaping what we know today as the Metaverse. These platforms enable users to embody different avatars, chat and communicate while freely navigating a wide-variety of virtual environments (VEs) that allowed diverse types of interaction. However, unlike real experiences where groups can collaborate and do different activities as they move from one place to another while maintaining social contact, virtual environments still miss features to preserve social presence and collaboration along navigation. In our work we aim to study group navigation, including both the notions of “going as a group inside one virtual environment” and “going as a group from one environment to a completely different one”. We will therefore design and evaluate new techniques and models of navigation and interaction methods that preserve the sense of group and collaboration.
title: Majd BITAR

content:
PhD student at Université de Lorraine, PErSEUs City: Metz Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0006-4353-5170 Supervisor: Stéphanie Fleck Thesis Title # Exploration de la tangibilisation de la présence socio-émotionnelle et de son influence sur les interactions et l’expérience d’apprentissage au cours de sessions d’apprentissage/travail collaboratif
Abstract # Empirical research has shown that collaboration involves an intense process of negotiation, conflicting points of view, constant interaction with others and shared work artefacts. This process presents socio-emotional challenges that require the individuals to have regulatory skills. This regulation covers a broad process in which cognition, emotion, motivation and behavior are adapted according to the objectives, goals, interactions and norms of the individuals and the group. While collaborative processes require operational synchronization and coordination, they also need an awareness of the emotional state of group members to ensure a positive and motivating working climate. However, it is very common for members of a group not to be trained in emotional awareness. To encourage optimal collaboration, it is crucial to make both learners and their teachers aware of the importance of emotional presence in collaborative dynamics. In this context, training them to be more aware of the affects of the individuals making up the group and their influence on collaborative dynamics, therefore appears strategic. Using a design-based research methodology, the aim of this project is to study the expressions, sharing and influences of socio-emotional presence in a collaborative context, whether or not supported by a tangible interface embodying that presence.
title: Mathilde YOUSEFI

content:
PhD student at UTC City: Compiègne Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/nan Supervisor: Indira Thouvenin, Yann Coello Thesis Title # AVATOUCH – Coopération synchrone basée sur l’intention sociale pour la manipulation d’un objet virtuel en environnement virtuel collaboratif
Abstract # No abstract available.
title: Mattéo BORREGO

content:
PhD student at G-SCOP City: Grenoble Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2649-282X Supervisor: Frédéric Noël, Romain Pinquié Thesis Title # Exploring the potential of the Industrial Metaverse in Systems Architecting: hope beyond the hype?
Abstract # No abstract available.
title: Sergio Camilo MEDINA GALVIS

content:
PhD student at IMT Atlantique, Lab-STICC, UMR CNRS 6285 City: Nantes & Grenoble Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0002-6586-841X Supervisor: Cédric Fleury, Laurence Nigay Thesis Title # PhyDiViews – Collaboration in Hybrid Environments (AR, VR, desktop): Multi-Views with Different Physicalities and Dimensions
Abstract # In recent years, particularly following the COVID-19 pandemic and the development of mixed reality technologies, there has been an increase in research projects focusing on remote collaboration systems. In many collaborative scenarios, remote participants must explore multiple points of view to better understand the environment and address complex problems. This thesis aims to enhance remote collaboration by investigating methods for sharing and synchronising points of view across multiple views with different physicalities and dimensions. It also aims to evaluate the impacts of transitions between different views on users’ cognitive load and spatial awareness. To achieve this, we first introduce a notation, PV*, which describes the coupling and control of points of view in remote collaboration systems, enabling a clearer understanding of their relationships. Using the PV*, we will then explore new solutions for managing and sharing multiple points of view.
title: Thomaz FEVRE

content:
PhD student at Université Paris-Saclay, LISN, AMI&ILDA City: Paris Starting Year: 2024 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0007-4620-6441 Supervisor: Ouriel Grynszpan, Olivier Chapuis Thesis Title # Joint Actions in a Collaborative Digital Environments: co-gestures and a Sense of Agency
Abstract # This PhD thesis investigates how groups of three people coordinate actions and experience a sense of agency during collaborative interaction. While research in Cognitive Science and Human–Computer Interaction (HCI) has largely focused on dyadic collaboration, little is known about how coordination and control are distributed in small groups. The research examines collaborative tasks in which participants jointly manipulate shared objects through coordinated actions such as translation, rotation, and scaling, across both physical and digital environments. Task difficulty is manipulated by varying the level of uncertainty about partners’ goals, allowing the study of how increasing coordination demands affect behavior and subjective experience. The thesis combines behavioral measures (performance, errors, and completion time) with subjective measures of workload and sense of agency. By linking coordination dynamics with perceived control, this research aims to contribute to theories of joint action and to inform the design of interactive systems that better support multi-user collaboration.
title: Xinpei  ZHENG

content:
PhD student at Paris Saclay - IBISC, LS2N & LMEE City: Evry Starting Year: 2023 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0003-0014-5111 Supervisor: Grégory Turbelin, Amine Chellali, Cédric Dumas Thesis Title # Étude des interactions haptiques interpersonnelles entre avatars virtuels
Abstract # The ANR MASTERS project (2023–2027) aims to design innovative pedagogical methods for law enforcement officers, firefighters, and healthcare professionals. These methods seek to help trainees maintain effective self-control in all circumstances, including situations involving high levels of constraint and intense stress (of psychological, social, or other origins) associated with challenging and dynamic contexts.
Within this project, the contribution of our teams is to design and develop virtual environments, multisensory interfaces, and dynamic models that enable realistic training and situational immersion tailored to these specific contexts.
The objective of this PhD project is, in a first phase, to implement within the platform an approach that enables the dynamic handling of physical contacts in virtual environments and their restitution to learners through haptic devices, such as vests or full-body suits. To achieve this, a modular approach is proposed. This approach consists in considering each avatar or virtual object as an independent elementary entity, while the connections between entities are treated as white-box components capable of communicating with the digital models of the involved entities.
In a second phase, the project aims to investigate the impact of these physical interactions on learners’ situational experience. More specifically, building on our model of avatar fidelity in immersive environments and on our previous studies on haptic communication, we plan to examine the contribution of the haptic modality, in addition to the visual modality, to the sense of presence and the sense of co-presence. This work will constitute the first step toward the development of the concept of the multisensory avatar.
title: Manel BOUKLI-HACENE

content:
PhD student at CESI LINEACT City: Paris Starting Year: 2023 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0002-3392-5838 Supervisor: Stéphanie Buisine, Beatrice Biancardi, Mukesh Barange Thesis Title # Vers l’amélioration de l’intelligence collective en réalité virtuelle
Abstract # My doctoral research focuses on collective intelligence in collaborative virtual reality environments. The objective is to better understand how small teams coordinate, communicate, and perform complex tasks when working together in immersive digital spaces, particularly in professional and educational contexts.
Rather than viewing team performance only through final outcomes, the thesis adopts a process-oriented perspective, examining how collective intelligence emerges dynamically through interaction. Virtual reality is used as a controlled yet ecologically valid medium, enabling teams to share the same space, manipulate common objects, and collaborate under clearly defined constraints.
The research combines experimental VR scenarios with systematic observation of team behavior, allowing the study of coordination, participation, and collaboration patterns during task execution. These observations are then used to model team functioning at a higher level, linking interaction processes to overall effectiveness.
A broader goal of the thesis is to contribute to the development of computational and theoretical frameworks for collective intelligence that are applicable to real-world collaborative work. By bridging cognitive science, human–computer interaction, and immersive technologies, this work aims to inform both the scientific understanding of teamwork and the design of future collaborative VR systems.
title: Tristan LANNUZEL

content:
PhD student at Université Paris-Saclay, LISN, Team ARAI City: Paris Starting Year: 2023 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0001-3895-8797 Supervisor: Christian Sandor Thesis Title # InstaBeam: Instant Beaming into Immersive Viewing Environments
Abstract # In the context of fashion design, translating design sketches to sewable garments relies on patternmakers, who typically use specialized Computer-Aided Design (CAD) software. Our goal is to automate the process empowering designers to go directly from sketch to sewing patterns. We first conducted a formative study comprising story-interviews with designers and in-situ observations to identify design opportunities for such a system. The study revealed that designers require sketching interface. We then conducted an elicitation study to understand how garment editing commands are communicated using sketches. Through qualitative analysis of 850 sketch marks, we derived design guidelines towards the development of intelligent sketch-based interactive systems. Building on our design guidelines, we are developing a sketch-based interactive system for digital fashion design.
title: David MARUSCSÁK

content:
PhD student at Inria Bordeaux (BIVWAC), Université de Bordeaux City: Paris Starting Year: 2023 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0009-6322-1971 Supervisor: Arnaud Prouzeau, Anastasia Bezerianos, Martin Hachet Thesis Title # Conception d’outils pour la collaboration lors de travaux pratiques en réalité virtuelle
Abstract # This PhD project focuses on human–computer interaction, education, and remote collaboration, with the goal of exploring how immersive technologies can support collaborative learning. Because virtual environments cannot fully replicate real-world experiences, it is essential to consider both human activity and the specific capabilities and limitations of technology when designing collaborative systems. As a result, this research goes beyond simply transferring face-to-face educational practices into virtual settings. Instead, it seeks to identify and design new communication styles and collaboration strategies that are better suited to virtual education. Especially, this research focus on the impact of immersive technologies on collaborative behaviours.
title: Juliette LE MEUDEC

content:
PhD student at Université de Rennes, Centre INRIA de Rennes City: Rennes Starting Year: 2023 Grant: Support ORCID: [https://orcid.org/ 0009-0003-5713-0754](https://orcid.org/ 0009-0003-5713-0754) Supervisor: Anatole Lécuyer Thesis Title # Social Haptics : Study of Haptic Feedback for Promoting Positive Social Interactions in Virtual Reality
Abstract # The general context of the PhD relates to “shared virtual environments”, in which multiple users can interact by means of immersive technologies. An economic craze has recently developed on this topic, especially with the buzz around “metaverses”, these massively shared virtual worlds accessible using Virtual Reality (VR) headsets. Beyond entertainment and video games, the applications of shared virtual environments are varied and numerous, including: education, training, cultural heritage, medicine, etc. The objective of this PhD thesis is to study the role and interest of the haptic modality in collaborative scenarios for promoting positive social interactions in Virtual Reality. More precisely, the objective is to :
understand the role of haptic modality in collaborative scenarios, i.e. the identification of meaningful haptic cues and percepts for supporting group interactions (perception/cognitive science) and design, implement and evaluate new haptic devices (hardware & software) simulating tactile/force feedback to one or multiple users. For this aim, various kinds of haptic technologies will be considered, including wearable haptic devices, non-contact haptic stimulation (ultrasound), vibratory actuators, encountered-type haptics, pseudo-haptics, tangible elements, etc. But also different part of the body will be concerned for the touch (arm, shoulder, back, etc.). The challenges are both hardware and software: The device should be robust and flexible enough to support a wide range of scenarios. The successful candidate will explore the design space offered by such haptic displays and progressively determine which modalities are the most suitable for social interactions in VR, in order to either transmit positive interactions from participants, promote or moderate interventions, etc.
title: Sabrina TOOFANY

content:
PhD student at Université de Toulouse, IRIT, ELIPSE City: Toulouse Starting Year: 2023 Grant: Full ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0005-6887-0744 Supervisor: Marcos Serrano, Arnaud Prouzeau, Emmanuel Dubois Thesis Title # Collaboration in Hybrid Environments:co-defining collaborative spatial layouts
Abstract # My research focuses on remote collaboration between participants interacting in heterogeneous environments. Heterogeneous environments are made of a diversity of input and output modalities ranging from desktop computers to advanced mixed reality devices. Heterogeneous environments allow the distribution of content and data among devices, according to each person’s role. Such environments can be distributed in different sites. However, distance and differences between heterogeneous environments may hinder collaboration. To address these barriers, we first focused on the identification and characterization of the asymmetries that can arise between users’ interactions across collaborative heterogeneous systems. To this end, we conducted a systematic literature review of asymmetric collaborative systems, coding their properties, including the interaction spaces, their input and output modalities, and shared feedback. This work led to the definition of the dimensions of asymmetrical collaboration. Second, we are now exploring the development of interaction techniques that promote remote workspace awareness and effective means of communication despite asymmetries between environments. To this end, we are exploring the use of a World-in-Miniature (WIM) in Augmented Reality (AR) representing the remote heterogeneous environment. This WIM provides awareness cues about the remote collaborators’ activities (cone of vision, device used, hands gestures, positions, alerts) and also constitutes a communication interface (screen-sharing, remote pointing). The AR headset used to display the WIM allows the user to keep their hands free and easily access their own devices.
title: Victor  BRÉHAULT

content:
PhD student at ENIB, Lab-STICC, Équipe INUIT City: Brest Starting Year: 2023 Grant: Support ORCID: https://orcid.org/0009-0004-5968-8361 Supervisor: Ronan Querrec, Sébastien Kubicki, Charlotte Hoareau Thesis Title # COLLABORATION TEMPS RÉEL DANS DES ESPACES HYBRIDES MELANT ENVIRONNEMENTS VIRTUELS ET INTERFACES TANGIBLES
Abstract # My PhD focuses on the design and evaluation of immersive systems for asymmetric remote collaboration, in contexts involving complex and critical tasks (e.g., a remote expert on Earth guiding an isolated operator on Mars). It is situated within the fields of Human-Computer Interaction and Virtual Reality.
The main objective is to investigate how different interaction modalities and immersive display setups can support the modification of structured procedures. Following a user-centered approach, this thesis proposes both situated and non-situated interfaces in VR that enable visualization and modification of a procedure.
In this thesis, we compare three interaction modalities (controller-based interaction, gesture-based interaction, and tangible object manipulation on an interactive table) combined with two visualization modalities (situated and non-situated) for modifying complex procedures in a virtual reality environment (within a CAVE). Through controlled experimental protocols, this work aims to empirically assess how these combinations influence task performance, cognitive workload, and user experience.
